Yes, block 0 offset is a special case to "bootstrap" the first offset when the `headers_offsets_file` is initially empty, otherwise `dynamic_header_offset` will not work. 

Check how the `fs_update_header_offsets` calculates the offset of block N based on the header size of block N-1.

One pythonic "fix" could be:

```
try:
    pos = (height - 1) * 8
    offset, = unpack('<Q', self.headers_offsets_file.read(pos, 8))
    return offset
except struct.error as e:
    if height == 0:
        return 0
    else:
        raise Exception('Header offset not found: {}'.format(height))
```

Another alternative is to check and write the block 0 offset when creating the `headers_offsets_file`.

It's definitely an import change! But what about making the change here: https://github.com/spesnova/dd-trace-py/blob/8b677483352c81cdaf773d1ba273f76d538d65cd/ddtrace/contrib/django/conf.py#L25-L26 ?

What I mean is that I prefer ENV < hard-coded settings, so that `DATADOG_TRACE ` settings are not overridden by env variables if set. In that way, you can just:
```python
DEFAULTS = {
    'AGENT_HOSTNAME': os.environ.get("DATADOG_TRACE_AGENT_HOSTNAME", 'localhost'),
    'AGENT_PORT': os.environ.get("DATADOG_TRACE_AGENT_PORT", 8126),
    # ...
}
```

This translates in having defaults as env vars, unless specified otherwise. 

Also, consider that `tracer.writer.api.port` must be an `int` otherwise the connection string will not work. Reference this PR: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/pull/140

I know we've talked about the API quite a bit. I wonder if time has come to *require* and astropy.table as input. They are not hard to make if you have something that's array like already. Most users will use this as part of the pipeline anyway and the other functions in photutils return astropy.table.Table objects.
In my mind, the "array like" is a left-over from the beginning of photutils when the design was not quite there for the Table structure. But now, that's all done.
I bet that photutils total line count can be decreased by 5% if we just use Table everywhere, because if  will save us a lot of if .. else.. . Also, it's safer and easier to set up (was the order in the array x,y, flux or was it flux, x, y ?).

I understand that existing photutils methods want to be backwards compatible, but once we write something new, we could decide to allow and and only one type on input in line with the "Zen of Python": 
```
>>> import this
The Zen of Python, by Tim Peters

Beautiful is better than ugly.
Explicit is better than implicit.
Simple is better than complex.
Complex is better than complicated.
Flat is better than nested.
Sparse is better than dense.
Readability counts.
Special cases aren't special enough to break the rules.
Although practicality beats purity.
Errors should never pass silently.
Unless explicitly silenced.
In the face of ambiguity, refuse the temptation to guess.
There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.
Although that way may not be obvious at first unless you're Dutch.
Now is better than never.
Although never is often better than *right* now.
If the implementation is hard to explain, it's a bad idea.
If the implementation is easy to explain, it may be a good idea.
Namespaces are one honking great idea -- let's do more of those!
```
Tables are clearly more explicit and they there would be one and only one way.

```python

In [2]: mask = np.random.rand(5, 5) > 0.5

In [3]: r, c = np.nonzero(mask) + 1.5
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-036d7e86f56f> in <module>()
----> 1 r, c = np.nonzero(mask) + 1.5

TypeError: can only concatenate tuple (not "float") to tuple
```

I'm wondering if it might be better to remove the `required` prop entirely, and let the outer form handle it like other validation.

On one hand, the prop is very convenient shorthand because it's a common pattern. On the other hand, it makes the textbox more complex by adding a "special" validation path.

My thinking is that in the longer-term, it would be better to accumulate a set of helper validation functions that can be imported and re-used at the form level rather than the textbox level. They would be able to return specific messages depending on their content.

E.g.:

* `required('')` might return `This field is required`
* `validUsername('r')` might return `A username must have at least two characters`
* `validUsername('&&&&&')` might return `A username can only have letters and numbers`

There are three possible outcomes when we encounter either `HTTPError` or `OSError`:

1. _retry_ the same file again
2. _skip_ the file and try the next one
3. _fail_ the entire transfer

| Exception | Condition | Outcome |
|--|--|--|
| `HTTPError` | `e.code == 404` | skip |
| `HTTPError` | `e.code in (502, 503, 504, 521, 522, 523, 524)` | retry |
| `HTTPError` | `else` | fail |
| `OSError` | `e.errno == errno.ENOENT` | skip |
| `OSError` | `else` | fail |

Actually, 32 bit float types can EXACTLY represent integer values up to around 8 million (and down to around -8 million). This is because 32 bit floats have 23 bits of mantissa, so rounding doesn't start to occur for integer values until 2^23 (~8 million).

So in fact,
 float(1.0) == double(1.0)

But,
 float(0.1) != double(0.1)

This is so because 0.1, while it is exactly represented in base-10, it has no exact representation in base-2. Much like how the fraction 1/9 cannot be represented exactly in base-10, but in base-9 it can.

> View change causes GC so if you are in the same view you only need pp_seq_no.
And what if you are in the next view and you have not ordered but already created 3pc messages in queues (e.g. sentPrePrepares)?

Let's say we ordered 1 message in view 0: last_ordered = (0,1). View change happened.
In the next viewNo=1 we sent new message, 3pc batch (1,1) was formed but view change happened again before it was ordered. last_ordered is still (0,1), so GC will remove that batch because it's ppSeqNo not more than last_ordered.ppSeqNo.
For now GC ignores viewNo and relative to view_change, but last_ordered_3pc is not.

I feel that we should keep the core of `IGangaFile` as simple as possible and that means both `get` and `put` doing one simple, predictable thing:

- `get()` copy all files which match `namePattern` from remote storage to `localDir`
- `put()` copy all files which match `namePattern` from `localDir` to remote storage

For `LocalFile` there's no difference between `locaDir` and remote storage so `get()` and `put()` should effectively be no-ops. If these functions always do this one predictable thing then we can depend on them and blindly call them on arbitrary `IGangaFile` instances (once we've ascertained whether it's an input or an output file).

If a user has a `DiracFile` which they want to be downloaded locally to some arbitrary location (e.g. `/home/person/my_analysis/data`) then they should set `DiracFile(..., localDir='/home/person/my_analysis/data')`.

Another confusion is that `IGangaFiles` do not represent files, they are instead serving a strange dual purpose as 1) declarative configuration (putting a `LocalFile` in `outputfiles` tells Ganga to run some different code path and do different things to if it were a `DiracFile`) and 2) a direction-undefined data pipe. One end of the pipe has some data (you must interpret the set schema attributes to work out which) and `get`/`put` push files through it. It can represent multiple files

This was my original implementation, however I ended up scraping it for a few (minor) reasons:

* To me it looked kind of weird with multiline strings (see example below).
* Using `assert_capture` for single-line and regular `capture` for multiline seemed inconsistent.
* There are other kinds of asserts (e.g. `assert "something" in capture`) which are more natural with the 'assert after' approach.

These aren't huge concerns, but there should actually be a minimal number of captures after the changes, so I don't think the line saving would be worth the inconsistency. I may be able to get rid of a few more of the captures -- I'll do another pass and see where `std::cout` can be replaced.

```python
with assert_capture("""
    Allocating child.
    Releasing child.
"""):
    p.addChild(Child())
    gc.collect()
 ```

I don't think this is a good pattern, where you set some data to a bad value, run a test, then set the data back to a good value for the next test. In particular with this series of tests, because you are asserting there should be a TypeError but with no further details, if you happen to make a mistake setting the value back to a good value, you may not be testing what you expect, as the code may be failing due to your incorrectly set "good" values rather than the expected bad value. This is the reasoning behind the general recommentation to run only a single test in each testing function, so that all state will be reset and you don't have to worry about state from one test contaminating the next test.

One possible approach here would be to write a helper function that creates an object that has all of the fields filled out with good data:
  d = initWithGoodData()  // I'm sure you can come up with a better name
  d.return_type = ['integer']   // bad data for this field
  with self.assertRaisesRegexp(TypeError, 'return_type'):
    google.datalab.bigquery.UDF('test_udf', d.code, d.return_type, d.params, d.language, d.imports);
Then you can put each of these tests into a separate test function.
